critique.summary: The article describes the author's experience creating a small compiler kernel called GDSL, which includes a Lisp subset implemented in 500 lines and a C subset in 1500 lines. The author reflects on how modern compilers are large and complex, and contrasts this with their minimalist approach that focuses on essential features and simplicity.
mainPoints: Modern compilers are typically very large, with millions of lines of code.
The author created a small compiler kernel (GDSL) with a Lisp subset in 500 lines and a C subset in 1500 lines.
The small compiler is functional and fast enough to be useful despite its minimal size.
The author questions what contributes to the large size of modern compilers and suggests it is due to complexity, 'seams,' 'landscapes,' and 'kludges.'
The project is ongoing, with the author planting the 'first seeds' for further development.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the size of modern compilers being in the millions of lines are generally accurate, as large compilers like GCC and LLVM are indeed very complex and extensive. The author's claim of implementing a Lisp subset in 500 lines and a C subset in 1500 lines is plausible for minimal subsets focusing on core features, though without external verification or demonstration, the performance and completeness cannot be fully assessed. The reflections on compiler complexity and tradeoffs are subjective but reasonable observations. Overall, the article is a personal project description rather than a rigorous technical report, and the factual claims about line counts and approach are credible but not independently verified.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed technical documentation or benchmarks to verify the performance and completeness of the compiler subsets.
It is unclear which features of C and Lisp are supported or omitted in these minimal subsets.
The article lacks external sources or comparisons to other minimal compiler projects.
The subjective terms like 'seams,' 'landscapes,' and 'kludges' are not defined or explained in detail.
The project appears to be in early stages, so claims about usefulness and stability may evolve.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the GDSL project, a small kernel implementing Lisp and C subsets, with comments clarifying its scope, sharing related projects, and questioning technical details like code generation and compiler size.

comments.author: ptspts
comments.text:
GDSL is written in C++ with use of STL, templates and lambdas, so it's 2600 lines of such C++ source code. There is no self-hosting: neither the LISP compiler nor the C compiler can compile itself. No operating system is implemented, the word kernel in the title means something else.

FYI Here is a 700-line subset-of-C compiler which can compile itself: https://github.com/valdanylchuk/xcc700 . FYI The linker and the libc are not included.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, factual clarifications about the project, correcting misconceptions about self-hosting and the meaning of 'kernel' in this context.

comments.author: xendo
comments.text: I thought you needed 100k lines of code for a C compiler. https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-c-compiler
comments.analysis: This comment references an external source to highlight the typical complexity of C compilers, providing useful context though not directly about GDSL.

comments.author: QuadmasterXLII
comments.text: where does it generate assembly assembly / llvm ir / machine code? I poked around and didn’t immediately spot this, or even which it targets.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid technical question about the project's code generation capabilities, indicating a gap in the provided information.

comments.author: akkartik
comments.text:
Looks interesting. The next step may be to show some little fun examples built with it.

Here's my similar project from a few years ago, in case you want to compare notes:

https://github.com/akkartik/mu

https://akkartik.name/akkartik-convivial-20200607.pdf
comments.analysis: This comment is constructive and offers a related project for comparison, though it does not provide direct factual information about GDSL.

comments.author: WalterGR
comments.analysis: This comment provides a useful resource link but adds little analysis or critique.
